plan.title: Hybrid MIL-Autoencoder Framework for Anomaly Detection in Whale Vocalizations
plan.abstract: This research addresses the challenge of accurately detecting anomalies in whale vocalizations, a critical task for marine biodiversity monitoring. Current methods exhibit high false normal classification rates and struggle with temporal localization, particularly in diverse acoustic environments. We propose a novel Hybrid MIL-Autoencoder Framework that leverages the strengths of Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) and Neural Network Autoencoder (NN-AE) to enhance anomaly detection capabilities. The approach involves developing a hybrid model architecture, which will be trained and evaluated on comprehensive whale vocalization datasets sourced from varied marine soundscapes. This model aims to surpass existing solutions by optimizing hyperparameters for real-time application, thereby reducing false normal rates and improving temporal anomaly localization. Our research is expected to contribute significantly to the field by providing a robust, adaptive tool for acoustic detection and real-time tracking of whale vocalizations, ultimately aiding in the conservation efforts of these vital marine species.

introduction.background: Anomaly detection in whale vocalizations is vital for monitoring marine biodiversity and understanding whale behaviors. Traditional models often exhibit high false normal rates and struggle with accurately localizing anomalies in time, especially within diverse and challenging acoustic environments. To address these issues, this research proposes a novel Hybrid MIL-Autoencoder Framework that combines Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) and Neural Network Autoencoder (NN-AE). MIL offers a robust approach for weakly supervised learning, allowing models to learn from datasets where only a subset of instances are labeled. Meanwhile, NN-AE is renowned for its capabilities in anomaly detection through reconstruction error analysis.
introduction.problem_statement: Current anomaly detection models in whale vocalizations suffer from high false normal rates and poor temporal localization in diverse marine soundscapes.
research_questions: RQ1: How can a hybrid MIL-Autoencoder framework reduce false normal rates in whale vocalization anomaly detection?
RQ2: What improvements in temporal localization accuracy can be achieved through this hybrid model?
RQ3: How does the hybrid model perform across different marine soundscapes compared to existing models?
objectives: Develop a hybrid MIL-Autoencoder model architecture.
Train the model on comprehensive whale vocalization datasets.
Evaluate model performance in diverse marine environments.
Compare the model's performance with existing approaches.
Optimize model hyperparameters for real-time application.

methodology.overview: This research methodology is structured into phases, each focusing on a critical component of the Hybrid MIL-Autoencoder Framework. The approach combines MIL for weakly supervised learning with NN-AE for anomaly detection, utilizing state-of-the-art techniques for feature extraction, model training, and evaluation.

phases.name: Phase 1: Data Preparation
phases.description: Collect and preprocess the whale vocalization datasets, ensuring they are suitable for training and evaluation of the hybrid model.
steps: Step 1: Collect comprehensive whale vocalization datasets from publicly available sources and marine research institutes.
Step 2: Perform data cleaning to remove noise and irrelevant information using techniques such as bandpass filtering and noise reduction.
Step 3: Normalize the datasets using standard normalization techniques to ensure consistency across all samples.
Step 4: Split the datasets into training, validation, and test sets with an 80/10/10 ratio.
Step 5: Apply data augmentation techniques to increase dataset diversity and robustness.
expected_outputs: Cleaned and normalized whale vocalization datasets.
Augmented datasets ready for model training.

phases.name: Phase 2: Model Development
phases.description: Develop the hybrid MIL-Autoencoder architecture that integrates MIL and NN-AE techniques.
steps: Step 1: Design the architecture of the hybrid model, incorporating MIL for weakly supervised learning and NN-AE for anomaly detection.
Step 2: Implement the model using a deep learning framework such as TensorFlow or PyTorch.
Step 3: Integrate feature extraction techniques such as STFT and Mel-spectrogram for input processing.
Step 4: Implement attention-based instance selection to enhance MIL capabilities.
Step 5: Incorporate residual blocks in the autoencoder for improved reconstruction.

phases.name: Phase 3: Model Training and Optimization
phases.description: Train the hybrid model on the prepared datasets and optimize hyperparameters for enhanced performance.
steps: Step 1: Train the hybrid model on the training dataset using AdamW optimizer.
Step 2: Evaluate the model on the validation set to monitor performance and adjust hyperparameters.
Step 3: Implement blocked cross-validation to ensure robustness across different data segments.
Step 4: Use focal binary cross-entropy loss to address class imbalance.
Step 5: Optimize hyperparameters using grid search for real-time efficiency.

phases.name: Phase 4: Evaluation and Comparison
phases.description: Evaluate the model's performance in diverse marine soundscapes and compare it with existing anomaly detection models.
steps: Step 1: Test the model on the test dataset and record performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
Step 2: Perform temporal anomaly localization assessment using AU-PRO and ROC metrics.
Step 3: Compare the model's results against existing models using benchmark datasets.
Step 4: Conduct statistical analysis to determine the significance of performance improvements.
expected_outputs: Performance metrics for the hybrid model.

methodology.data_collection: Data will be sourced from public marine research databases and organizations that provide whale vocalization recordings.
methodology.analysis_approach: Performance will be analyzed using statistical tests to compare against benchmarks and evaluate improvements in anomaly detection and localization.

expected_contributions: Reduction in false normal classification rates in whale vocalization anomaly detection.
Improved temporal localization accuracy in diverse acoustic environments.
A robust framework for real-time anomaly detection in marine soundscapes.

milestones: Completion of data collection and preprocessing.
Implementation of model in deep learning framework.

potential_challenges.challenge: High variability in whale vocalization datasets.
potential_challenges.mitigation: Use data augmentation and normalization techniques to enhance dataset robustness.

potential_challenges.challenge: Computational resource constraints for real-time application.
potential_challenges.mitigation: Optimize model architecture and hyperparameters for efficiency and consider cloud-based processing solutions.
Incorporate explainable AI techniques to enhance model transparency.

references: Methodologies from papers such as 2502.01543, 2502.20838, 2502.01920, 2502.18328, and 2503.11115 inspired the integration of MIL and NN-AE in this research.

methods: Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise (DBSCAN)
Feature engineering including 'duration at the same station', 'number of detections', 'number of days detected', 'number of unique stations', 'consecutive missing stations'
Data pre-processing including cleaning, normalizing, and splitting into training, validation, and test sets
Resampling strategy to adjust irregular sampling
methodology_inspirations.relevance: The methodologies used in this study, particularly the application of Neural Network Autoencoder (NN-AE) for anomaly detection in large-scale telemetry data, can inspire new research in other domains where large datasets and anomaly detection are critical. The study's approach to feature engineering and resampling strategies could be adapted for other time-series data challenges, providing a framework for improving model performance in detecting anomalies.

These methods, particularly the use of Multiple Instance Learning and dual-stream architectures, can inspire new research in weakly supervised learning for other domains with large, unannotated datasets. The attention-based instance selection and feature fusion techniques could be applied to improve detection and localization tasks in various fields such as medical imaging, environmental monitoring, and video analysis.

The methodologies in this paper, particularly the integration of autoencoders with noise contrastive estimation and the innovative use of composite features, provide a robust framework for anomaly detection. These methods could inspire new research in improving anomaly detection systems by combining different feature representations and optimizing noise distributions, potentially leading to more accurate and computationally efficient models.

methods: Evaluation metrics: F1, ROC, AU-PRO, Faithfulness on Spectra
methodology_inspirations.relevance: These methods highlight the potential of adapting visual anomaly detection techniques to audio domains, offering improved explainability and localization of anomalies. This cross-domain adaptation could inspire new research in developing more interpretable machine learning models across various data types and applications, particularly in enhancing the explainability of anomaly detection systems.

methods: Whisper for Mel spectrogram feature extraction
Multilayer perceptron (MLP) trained on the Aff-Wild2 dataset
Cross-modal alignment and fusion module with multi-scale self-attention and adaptive sliding windows
Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) for temporal modeling
Global and local views for feature extraction
Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) for audio preprocessing
Global average pooling for visual feature extraction
methodology_inspirations.relevance: The methodologies used in this paper, such as the integration of ConvNeXt and Whisper for multimodal feature extraction, and the use of advanced cross-modal fusion techniques, could inspire new research in improving the robustness of emotion recognition systems in real-world settings. The combination of global-local views and temporal modeling with TCNs offers a comprehensive approach to capturing both static and dynamic aspects of facial expressions, which could be further explored in other domains requiring detailed temporal analysis.
